title: India-Canada CEPA Terms of Reference Finalized: $50 Billion Trade Target by 2030
lede: India and Canada have finalized the Terms of Reference for a Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement during Canadian PM Carney's visit, establishing a framework to nearly double bilateral trade to USD 50 billion by 2030.

sections.context: The finalization of the CEPA Terms of Reference during the Canadian Prime Minister's February 27–March 2, 2026 visit to India marks a significant escalation in bilateral economic engagement. The ToR provides the essential framework, frequency, and modalities to guide formal negotiations toward an ambitious, balanced, and mutually beneficial agreement covering trade in goods and services alongside other agreed policy areas. This development follows years of strained diplomatic relations between the two nations, suggesting a deliberate recalibration of the relationship toward economic cooperation. The agreement targets acceleration of progress toward achieving USD 50 billion in bilateral trade by 2030—a figure that represents a substantial expansion from current levels and reflects both nations' commitment to deepening economic ties.
sections.threads: This CEPA negotiation sits within a broader strategic reset between New Delhi and Ottawa, visible in the [[india-ca-canada-india-strategic-partnership-renewed-under-one-ea-2026|Canada-India Strategic Partnership Renewed Under 'One Earth']] framework and signals the beginning of what analysts are characterizing as an [[india-ca-india-canada-cepa-negotiations-launch-economic-reset-af-2026|economic reset following years of diplomatic turbulence]]. The economic partnership is being pursued alongside parallel institutional cooperation, including the [[india-ca-india-canada-cepa-framework-launched-50-billion-trade-t-2026|India-Canada CEPA Framework announcement]], suggesting a multi-track approach to stabilizing the relationship. Additionally, India and Canada have launched a trilateral technology and innovation partnership with Australia, indicating that New Delhi is positioning Canada within a broader Indo-Pacific economic architecture rather than treating the relationship in isolation.
sections.signal: Analysts should monitor the pace and substance of CEPA negotiation rounds scheduled under the finalized ToR. The next critical checkpoint will be whether both nations maintain negotiating momentum given historical sensitivities in bilateral relations. The USD 50 billion target by 2030 will serve as a quantifiable metric of success; current trade volumes and annual growth rates will determine feasibility. Watch for sectoral priorities—whether India pushes on services and IT while Canada emphasizes agricultural and resource exports—as these will reveal the true contours of the agreement. The trilateral technology partnership with Australia deserves attention as it may create either complementarity or competition within CEPA discussions. Any disruption in diplomatic relations could derail these economic talks, making political stability a prerequisite for the agreement's conclusion.
